author: @iandawson9050
text: I got this tip many years ago when I managed a team of staff doing msintenave in a south London hospital. When drilling through glass use a masonry bit slow speed build a small dam around the hole and fill with white spirit.

author: @Gwydion67
text:
Thanks Luke!
Besides that reminds me of what an old bloke I am. Made this "discovery" literally a century ago... 🤣😜

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hahaha awesome! Cheers mate! :)

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
That is excellent. I know it these days but I wish i would have seen this video 30 years ago. I did not know at that age and it certainly would have helped to avoid many mistakes.
You could easily make a few other videos on the subject. Here are some facts I learned the hard way :

One needs lubricant to drill metal. Oils for steel, I believe denaturised alcolhol is used for aluminium. ( I'm not sure about the last. Ask your dad. )
Low rpm is needed for steel or other hard material.
You need to stop and cool you drill in water for some materials. Just going on destroys the drill bit.
One really needs a hammer drill to make a hole in hard concrete. An impact drill is not going to cut it.
Sharpening drill's is a real skill. I believe there are tools to help with that. I have seen people doing a reasonable job by hand on a grinding stone. I tried and mess up most of the time.
Soft chalk based stone should be drilled with a bit suitable for wood.

Fun fact : The metal on the stone and concrete drills is called "widia"  It's short for "wie diamant" which translates from german to english as "like a diamond". It's that hard  ...

author: @MrBunkerstudio
text: Well done Luke!! I like the fact that the video was short and to the point, you started at the beginning! Don't worry about the comments ref all the drills you missed!! I love the way you delt with the 3 !!

text: keep your hands still

author: @rastamanralph6670
text:
I bet you didn't know you can get left handed drill bits? 
I didn't either until a couple of months ago!
I was watching a video about how to extract snapped studs in crucial places like the cylinder head of a car after trying to remove exhaust manifold studs.
I hope this will help you and others.👍😁
Luckily I grew up with a father who did diy jobs around the house and garden so I was taught at an early age the difference between drill bits but he didn't know about left handed bits and probably still doesn't so I might just teach him something today! 😉💛

author: @htmagic
text: Luke, you forgot to mention diamond drills, hollow drills (magnetic drill press), step drills, burrs, and other types of bits. Original drills were bit and brace and mainly used on wood.

author: @CanadairCL44
text: Good video Luke! I know somebody who was trying to drill a hole in an internal wall, using  wood drill bit in a hammer drill !  He couldn't understand why he was getting nowhere until I told him what he was doing wrong.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hahahaha that sounds like me lol. I honestly didn't know different drill bits were used for different jobs. Now I do it has made life easier lol. Cheers mate! :)

author: @pulesjet
text: Shaking the bits around made viewing them a challenge Sir.  Diamond bits missing.   Diamond drill thru glass well if they stay wet.  Also work on extremely hard metals.

replies.author: @joeharter7498
replies.text: I'm sure there are 1001 different types of drill bits but happy to hear that you are partial to diamond.

author: @ChopShopGarage
text: Good simple explanation. I would just add that the material of the drill bits is also different. As an example, the masonry drill bit has a diamond tip to resist drilling through concrete, bricks, etc.

replies.author: @Edsbar
replies.text: The really cheap masonry drills have tips that are made of cheese.

replies.author: @CanadairCL44
replies.text: Actually the tip is usually Tungsten Carbide. I used to work in a factory that made them.

replies.author: @ChopShopGarage
replies.text: @CanadairCL44 I stand corrected then. 😊

replies.author: @joeharter7498
replies.text: Could you explain a little further why a masonry drill bit resists drilling through masonry?

replies.author: @Edsbar
replies.text: @CanadairCL44 The tps of the really cheap ones are made of cheese carbide and aren't straight.
